label: no
cot_prefix: Okay, let me try to figure out this question. So the story is about an employer who's settling accounts with his children's governess, Julia. He starts off saying they agreed on thirty rubles a month, but she says forty. He insists on thirty and then starts deducting various amounts for Sundays, holidays, days the kids were sick, her having a toothache, breaking a teacup, the kid climbing a tree and tearing his jacket, the maid stealing shoes, and some other payments. Eventually, he ends up giving her eleven rubles, but then gets angry when she says "merci" because he feels he's cheated her. Then he gives her eighty rubles instead and reflects on how easy it is to crush the weak. The question is asking why the employer made all those criticisms about Julia's work. The options are A to D. Let me go through the story again to pick out the key points. First, the employer brings Julia into his study to settle accounts. He mentions that even though she probably needs money, she won't ask for it. He starts with the agreed salary, then subtracts various days and incidents. However, Julia doesn't argue any of these points, even when the math seems off. For example, he subtracts nine Sundays, three holidays, etc., but Julia doesn't correct him. Then he adds more deductions for the broken teacup, the torn jacket, stolen shoes, etc. At the end, after giving her eleven rubles, he gets angry because she thanks him, realizing that he's actually cheated her. Then he gives her the full eighty rubles and reflects on the weakness of the governess. So, the employer's criticisms (deductions for her work) seem to be part of a deliberate effort to pay her less. But then he gives her more in the end as a "cruel lesson" to teach her to protest. The key here is that the employer's main motivation in making those deductions wasn't necessarily because she actually did those things (though some might be true), but to pay her less. Or maybe it was a test to see if she would stand up for herself. Looking at the options:
